metadata.summary: Implementation plans bridge design docs and executable tasks by transforming architectural decisions into ordered, token-budgeted work units with wave analysis for parallel execution.

content.title: Writing Implementation Plans
content.summary: An implementation plan bridges design docs and executable tasks by transforming architectural decisions (AD-X) into ordered, token-budgeted work units optimized for LLM agent execution. The 100k token rule ensures each task fits within a focused context window. Wave analysis identifies parallel execution opportunities. Every task must trace to an AD-X or FR-X — orphan tasks are a quality gate failure.

why.p: Without an implementation plan, agents execute tasks in arbitrary order, creating merge conflicts and dependency violations. Wave analysis enables 1.8-3.5x parallelization speedup. Token budgets prevent context window exhaustion, which is the most common cause of incomplete or broken agent outputs.

Planning against an approved design reduces rework. Explicit dependencies prevent hidden coupling. Traceability ensures all design decisions are implemented and no extra work is invented without requirement backing.

list.item: After spec and design are both in Approved status.
When feature has 3+ tasks requiring ordered execution.
When design doc contains parallel-eligible work (Wave 1 / Wave 2 pattern).
When task token estimates exceed 30k (simple tasks may skip formal plan).
Before spec or design are approved — planning against unapproved docs creates rework.
For trivial single-file changes that need no coordination.
For bug fixes that touch one file and have no dependencies.
When design doc has no AD-X decisions (no design = no plan needed).

rule.statement: Every task must pass INVEST: Independent (can start after declared deps complete), Negotiable (implementation flexible), Valuable (traces to FR-X), Estimable (token estimate declared), Small (under 100k tokens), Testable (acceptance criteria are verifiable).
rule.rationale: Tasks that fail INVEST produce incomplete agent executions. The most common failures are: not Independent (hidden deps cause ordering conflicts), not Small (context exhaustion mid-task), not Testable (no way to validate completion).

antipattern.description: Task with acceptance criteria that say "works correctly" or "functions as expected".
antipattern.reason: Vague AC cannot be verified by a test or a quality gate check. Every AC must specify a concrete observable outcome: a status code, a file that exists, a database row, a rendered element.

rule.statement: Assign each task to a tier: Simple (under 30k, single file, clear pattern), Normal (30-60k, multiple files, some exploration), Complex (60-100k, many files, novel solution). Tasks over 100k must be split before execution begins.
rule.rationale: Agents degrade non-uniformly near context limits. The 100k rule provides a safe margin for most model families. Declaring a tier forces estimation before execution.

example.code:
### TASK-003: Create user authentication middleware
- **Complexity:** Normal - **Tokens:** ~45k - **AD-X:** AD-002 - **FR-X:** FR-005, FR-006 - **Depends on:** TASK-001 (user model), TASK-002 (JWT utility) - **Blocks:** TASK-005 (protected routes)
**Files:** - CREATE `src/middleware/auth.py` - MODIFY `src/app.py` — register middleware
**Acceptance Criteria:** 1. Given valid JWT, when request arrives, then `request.user` is populated. 2. Given expired JWT, when request arrives, then 401 response is returned. 3. Given missing Authorization header, then 401 is returned.

section.name: required-task-fields
section.p: Every task in the implementation plan must declare these fields:

list.item: Task ID (TASK-NNN format)
Title: action verb + target (e.g., "Create auth middleware")
Complexity tier and token estimate
AD-X and FR-X trace references (min 1 each)
Depends on (explicit list or "None")
Blocks (what tasks need this to complete)
Files with action (CREATE / MODIFY / DELETE) and purpose
Acceptance criteria (min 2, verifiable)

rule.statement: Build the dependency graph from design.md, not from the task list. For each file operation in design.md, ask: what must exist before this file can be created or modified? The answers are dependencies.
rule.rationale: Inferring dependencies from task descriptions introduces hidden assumptions. Grounding the DAG in design.md file operations produces a complete, objective dependency set.

rule.statement: Use only Finish-to-Start (FS) dependencies in implementation plans. Document the others (SS, FF) only if they provide a real parallelization opportunity — do not add them for completeness.
rule.rationale: Non-FS dependencies add complexity without benefit unless agents can actually exploit them. Most agent executors handle only sequential-within-wave execution.

rule.statement: Wave 1 = all tasks with no dependencies. Wave N = all tasks whose dependencies are entirely in Waves 1 through N-1. Repeat until all tasks are assigned. Tasks in the same wave can be executed in parallel.
rule.rationale: The wave algorithm is deterministic and reproducible. Agents and humans can verify it independently by inspecting the dependency list of each task.

example.code:
| Wave | Tasks | Parallel | Token Load | |------|-------|----------|------------| | 1 | TASK-001, TASK-002 | Yes | 25k + 20k | | 2 | TASK-003, TASK-004 | Yes | 45k + 35k | | 3 | TASK-005 | No | 50k |
Critical path: TASK-001 → TASK-003 → TASK-005 = 120k tokens

rule.statement: Identify the longest dependency chain by token sum. Prioritize optimization of critical path tasks — splitting or simplifying them reduces total execution scope more than optimizing off-path tasks.
rule.rationale: The feature cannot complete faster than its critical path. Off-path task optimization produces no schedule benefit for the overall feature execution.

antipattern.description: Agent declares all tasks as Wave 1 (no dependencies) to maximize apparent parallelism.
antipattern.reason: Missing dependencies cause merge conflicts and broken builds when parallel agents overwrite each other's files. Require dependency evidence from design.md for every "no dependencies" claim.

antipattern.description: Agent declares all tasks as depending on the previous one, producing a purely sequential plan.
antipattern.reason: LLMs default to sequential thinking. A purely sequential plan with more than 3 tasks likely contains false dependencies. Review each FS edge: does the downstream task actually need the upstream file to exist, or is it just logically related?

rule.statement: Every AD-X from design.md must have at least one corresponding task. Every FR-X from spec.md must trace to at least one task. Every task must trace to at least one AD-X or FR-X. Zero orphan tasks permitted.
rule.rationale: The 100% rule ensures no design decision is left unimplemented and no task was invented without requirement backing. It is the primary completeness check before execution begins.

rule.statement: Verify traceability by running: `grep -c "AD-" implementation-plan.md` and comparing to the AD-X count in design.md. Do the same for FR-X. Any gap must be resolved before the plan is approved.
rule.rationale: Manual counting is error-prone. The grep check takes 10 seconds and catches the most common oversight: a design decision that was noted but never assigned to a task.

list.item: spec.md status: Approved
design.md status: Approved
All AD-X decisions finalized (no "TBD" entries)
constitution.md read for project naming and testing standards
Completed plans in done/ reviewed for patterns

rule.statement: Do not write an implementation plan against a design doc with TBD architectural decisions. Resolve all TBDs in design.md first.
rule.rationale: Planning against unresolved decisions produces tasks that change as soon as the decision is made, wasting the planning work and creating confusion during execution.

antipattern.description: Task token estimate over 100k with no split.
antipattern.reason: Declare the task as "Split Required" and decompose into subtasks before proceeding. A 150k task is not one complex task — it is two or three tasks with a shared header.

antipattern.description: Task references another task in "Depends on" that does not exist in the plan.
antipattern.reason: Add the missing task or fix the reference. Missing dependency tasks are the second most common cause of execution failures after token overruns.
Restructure the dependency chain. Circular deps indicate a design problem: one of the two tasks needs to be split, or one dependency is false (logically related but not a real FS constraint).

antipattern.description: File structure section listing non-existent files or files that cannot be split as declared.
antipattern.reason: Verify every listed file against the actual codebase. Executor agents will either create orphaned files or fail when files they need do not exist.

rule.statement: When executing tasks in a wave, dispatch each task to a separate sub-agent or worktree. After all wave tasks complete, merge results to main branch before starting Wave N+1.
rule.rationale: Isolated sub-agents prevent merge conflicts during parallel execution. Sequential wave execution with parallel commits ensures dependency ordering.

rule.statement: Use wave-based token budget: sum all task tokens in a wave. If the total exceeds 400k, split the wave into smaller waves or split some tasks further.
rule.rationale: A single wave exceeding 400k tokens risks overwhelming orchestrator token budgets. Breaking waves ensures orchestrator agents have budget for coordination overhead.

section.name: implementation-plan-template-sections
section.p: A complete implementation-plan.md includes these sections:

list.item: Overview: feature summary, scope statement, success criteria.
Spec/Design References: links to approved spec.md and design.md.
Wave Table: summary of waves, parallelism, critical path.
Critical Path: longest dependency chain by token sum.
Task List: one TASK-XXX section per task with all required fields.
Traceability Matrix: table verifying FR-X → task and AD-X → task mappings.
Risks and Mitigations: known risks and how to handle them.

rule.statement: Before execution begins, verify: (1) all tasks pass INVEST, (2) all dependencies are explicit and grounded in design.md, (3) 100% traceability to FR-X and AD-X, (4) total tokens ≤ resource budget, (5) all AC are concrete and verifiable.
rule.rationale: Quality gates at plan approval prevent execution failures. Fixing issues at planning stage is much cheaper than discovering them during task execution.
